item.title: The Criterion Mobile Closet is Coming to the Toronto International Film Festival!
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded:
After wildly successful stops in multiple cities across the US, we’re making our first stop outside the country at the Toronto International Film Festival from September 4 - 7!
Find us on King St. across from the TIFF Lightbox — come in, explore the collection, and make your own Closet video! Sign up and receive updates and get all the important information you'll need to visit us: https://bit.ly/4fx4Gce

item.title: STEVEN SODERBERGH - Director
item.author: jamesdeakins@me.com (jamesdeakins@me.com)
item.content:encoded: SEASON 2 - EPISODE 153 - Steven Soderbergh - DirectorIn this extended episode of the Team Deakins Podcast, we speak with director Steven Soderbergh (BLACK BAG, TRAFFIC, OUT OF SIGHT). Steven credits his father for giving him the movie bug, and he shares how a young adulthood shooting short films and writing spec scripts in Louisiana led to the 8-years-in-the-making overnight success of his debut feature: SEX, LIES, AND VIDEOTAPE. We spend much of the episode discussing Steven’s insights into directing and filmmaking, and he opens up about his transition into shooting his own films under the alias Peter Andrews. Steven also reflects on directing OUT OF SIGHT, recalling how he ended up with the job, when a studio note protected him from his own instinct to be lean in the film’s edit, and why he felt the movie was at stake when he went to war to cast Jennifer Lopez. Steven also stresses the importance of character and reputation in the business, and he recalls the pitfalls of acting like an egomaniac that he witnessed while crewing in his early days. We also discuss the value of establishing a basic grammar and set of rules specific to the film one is making, and Steven shares how he tries to listen to what a movie wants “itself” to be.Recommended Viewing: OUT OF SIGHT This episode is sponsored by Aputure & Barco

item.title: Ari Aster’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The filmmaker returns to the Criterion Closet, where he praises seventies thrillers like THE PARALLAX VIEW and STRAW DOGS, talks about YOU CAN COUNT ON ME and how “no one writes dialogue like Kenneth Lonergan,” and connects TARGETS to his new film EDDINGTON.

item.title: Danny Boyle’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The filmmaker cites ALL THAT JAZZ as a major influence on modern editing, reveals the connection between MEMORIES OF UNDERDEVELOPMENT and TRAINSPOTTING, and shares his theory on why a director’s first film is always their best.

item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews Savages
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
In Borneo, at the edge of the tropical forest, Kéria is given a baby orangutan. With the forest under greater threat than ever, Kéria, Selaï and the little primate, Oshi, will have to face many obstacles.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews The Legend of Ochi
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
In a remote village on the island of Carpathia, a shy girl is raised to fear an elusive animal species known as ochi. But when she discovers a wounded baby ochi has been left behind, she escapes on a quest to bring him home.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.title: Why The Movies Will Never Feel The Same Again
item.author: Thomas Flight

item.title: LUCY PREBBLE - Playwright / Writer
item.author: jamesdeakins@me.com (jamesdeakins@me.com)
item.content:encoded: SEASON 2 - EPISODE 152 - Lucy Prebble - Playwright / WriterPlaywright and writer Lucy Prebble (SUCCESSION, I HATE SUZIE, ENRON) joins us in this episode of the Team Deakins Podcast. Lucy’s plays frequently incorporate the physical space of the stage in the telling of the story and involve the audience in engaging and creative ways, and we were excited to talk to her about storytelling both on stage and on screen. We learn how, after a bookish childhood, she eventually found the courage to have her own plays put on, and she reflects on her experiences running her own television show and working on others’. Lucy also wrote for the amply-budgeted video game DESTINY, and she shares (as much as she’s allowed to) how the writing process works for something with that many resources. Lucy also reveals the real-world events that inspired her plays ENRON and THE SUGAR SYNDROME, and we learn how she identifies the real human relationships at the heart these stories. We also discuss the value of empathy and deadlines for a writer, and Lucy makes a prediction for the future of the theatre in the digital age. Plus, we observe that many of history’s greatest writers were really just frustrated actors.This episode is sponsored by Barco & Aputure

item.title: Barbie Ferreira’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The actor selects favorite horror films like VIDEODROME and THE VANISHING, talks about connecting with the work of Gregg Araki, and recommends that everyone watch Todd Haynes’s SAFE.

item.title: Street Trash - re:View
item.author: RedLetterMedia
item.content:encoded: What's this? Jay is talking about another filthy garbage movie? What else is new? This time however, he is joined by Josh Robert Thompson, the world's foremost Street Trash expert. Together, they have the most thorough, thoughtful discussion of the underseen cult movie that anyone has ever had. Buckle up, prudes!

item.title: Tunde Adebimpe’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The musician and actor talks about what makes Stan Brakhage’s films “very punk rock,” reflects on working with Jonathan Demme, and selects favorite films such as BLACK GIRL and TRUE STORIES.

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews Narrow Road to the Deep North
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
Burma Railway in 1943 and across the Pacific during World War II, charts the cruelty of war, the tenuousness of life and the impossibility of love, as seen through the eyes of an Australian doctor and prisoner of war.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
Forced to balance their roles as heroes with the strength of their family bond, the Fantastic Four must defend Earth from a ravenous space god called Galactus and his enigmatic Herald, Silver Surfer.

item.title: The A24 Podcast | Creative Affair with Celine Song and Eva Victor
item.author: A24
item.content:encoded:
Today on The A24 Podcast, Celine Song (Materialists) and Eva Victor (Sorry, Baby,) the powerhouse filmmakers behind two of this Summer's best movies, go deep on hydration regimens, the grief/euphoria of releasing their first films, sharing an editor with Terrence Malick, bringing their whole humanity to the page and more. Available anywhere you listen & full video now on A24 YouTube!
Topics covered include: Martini celebrations, hydration regimens, learning the true meaning of the word ‘release,’ feeling visible but not seen, bringing your whole humanity to the words on a page, sharing an editor with Terrence Malick, the Mother/Sister/Daughter theory, the subtle differences between directing Pedro Pascal, Chris Evans and Dakota Johnson, alleviating grief by thinking about The Next Thing, desperately needing sleep, Celine going straight from the Oscars to the first Materialists scout, Eva’s intricate process directing a movie they also acted in, sparring of ideas, committing to subjectivity, and having bangs.

item.title: DAVE FREETH - Inventor of the Stabileye
item.author: jamesdeakins@me.com (jamesdeakins@me.com)
item.content:encoded: SEASON 2 - EPISODE 151 - Dave Freeth - Inventor of the StabileyeIn this episode of the Team Deakins Podcast, we speak with Dave Freeth, a recipient of a Scientific and Engineering Award by the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences for the invention of the Stabileye. The Stabileye is a remotely-operated, motorized camera stabilization system, and we used the equipment extensively on 1917 to pull the film off. Dave originally started out in an apprenticeship for watchmaking but soon found himself working for the Ministry of Defence until making the transition into the film industry. We asked Dave to come on the podcast to discuss the Stabileye’s inception and design, and he describes the underlying mechanics that not only power the Stabileye but other stabilization systems in use today. We talk about gyroscopes, satellites, lasers, and even gravity! He also shares how the Stabileye is adapting to newer camera models, and he reveals some surprising potential developments in sensor technology. We learn a lot in this episode, and we were excited to celebrate Dave’s recent recognition with him. This episode is sponsored by Aputure & Barco

item.title: The Criterion Mobile Closet in Los Angeles at the Aero Theatre
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: Back in June, the Criterion Mobile Closet made its second stop in Los Angeles, this time in Santa Monica at the Aero Theatre in partnership with American Cinematheque. Take a look back on our amazing weekend! Thank you to everyone who spent their time with us — we loved meeting you all and were thrilled by dedication to cinema and enthusiasm for community. Keep an eye open for future Mobile Closet announcements just around the corner.

item.title: Matthew Broderick’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The actor shares what he loves about Preston Sturges and Alfred Hitchcock, recommends BRIEF ENCOUNTER and the more intimate works of David Lean, and selects films featuring favorite actors like Claude Rains and Carole Lombard.

item.title: Best of the Worst: Star Slammer, Talos, and The Apple
item.author: RedLetterMedia
item.content:encoded:
Ahoy! It’s time for an epic Best of the Worst episode filled with magic, martial arts, and Menahem! Menahem Golan that is, of Cannon Films, as we celebrate the sci fi dystopian musical The Apple! We also watched some disgusting trash called Star Slammer about a women’s prison in space. It’s filth. Utter filth. What would Jesus do? And to round things out, we watch the ambitious no budget martial arts fantasy adventure film Talos. Look out everyone, it’s Talos!
Special thanks to Bear McCreary (https://bearmccreary.com) for joining us on this nightmare.
